speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Bring the tissues here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks, Duff.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. You're all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: East.

speaker: interviewee
text: East.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train just continued to move eastward. And the further east we traveled, the colder the weather became, and obviously, since we had no heat, the colder we became. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not sure exactly how many days we were on the train, must have been about three or four days possibly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Landscape was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all white, snow. It was cold outside, obviously. It was December. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on December the 12th, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we arrived on what was supposed to be our destination. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Quite a few people had gotten sick on the train. We had-- there was no medical help available. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To the best of my recollection, someone had died on a train. The word had filtered through, although not in a car where I was in. There were doctors available who were with us, part of the group of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1,000 people on that train. And on December the 12th, we arrived what was called our destination. Train stopped. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Doors opened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were in a open area with a train station on the railroad tracks, or off the railroad tracks, which read [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nobody, of course, knew what [NON-ENGLISH] was and where we were. But us, we got out of the train. And exiting the trains, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a lot of noise, a lot of commotion. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Get out of the train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: For the first time, I saw a group of SS man [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who were ordering the evacuation of the trains, of-- of the cars. And there was one particular man there who stood out above all of 'em. He was wearing a different type of a uniform, he's wearing a SS uniform, but he was wearing a different head gear, and he had his stars on his amulets or epaulets-- what is it?-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which designated him to be a higher ranking officer. He also had a dog with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Someone approached him to ask questions, where we were and what was going on. And after all, we were German citizens and we didn't-- didn't appreciate to be treated like he was treating us. And he took out his gun, and he shot that man right through the head, straight through the head. That was our first experience of the SS [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being as brutal as they turned out to be. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he had a megaphone with him. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he addressed us as [GERMAN]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's you pigs, pig Jews. If you don't do what you're told to do, if you don't behave like you're expected to behave, what happened to this man right now, it's going to happen to all of you, so just do what you're told to do and nothing will happen to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's our luggage? Looking around, we asked several people of the guards who were with us, who were on the trains. Well, the luggage car where luggage was in had mechanical problems and we had to disconnect it from the train. Your luggage is not here, but possibly we'll get it later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew better, we didn't. The luggage car was never hooked onto the train in Kassel. [BACKROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And our things, which were our worldly belongings, we no longer had. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very cold that day, must've been about 10:00, 11 o'clock in the morning. It was snowing. It was snowing so badly. I had never seen snow like that, although Gemunden, where I was born and raised, we had snow every winter. But it used to be a soft snow we used to have. It was snowing so hard. And it was so cold. And we were not prepared for that kind of weather. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put us in columns and told us, if you do what you're told to do, you're going to be all right. If not, what's going to-- what happened to this man, it's going to happen to you. And obviously, you know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not having [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] any idea how unhuman [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] humans can be-- and after all, they were Germans and so were we. Well, when I say Germans, my father fought in the Second World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: First World War.

speaker: interviewee
text: First World War. I'm sorry. My father fought in the First World War, was on the Russian front, was wounded. Had a-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] a cross, Iron Cross I believe it's called, for his bravery in action. My grandfather had been serving the Kaiser in 1890. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were as German as Germans-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as German as you can be. Sure, we were Jewish, but basically, you know, we're Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it took us a while to realize that it really didn't matter who you were, where you were, if you served your-- your country or-- or whatever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was hard to believe, how to understand, you know. Anyhow, we marched. We went into this column. And it was icy on the road and it's still snowing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we must have marched probably for an hour and a half through the snow and ice. And it was so cold that some people just were left by the wayside. Older people couldn't make it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were hearing shots. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we realized that those people who were laying on the wayside, who couldn't make it, they were just shot. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Approximately after a march of probably an hour and a half so, we arrived at a gate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were marching through [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a residential area. It was a poorer neighborhood, but it still and all was residential and people were standing on the sidewalks and looking at us. Nobody said anything. But eventually, we wound up at this gate. The gate opened up. Column was stopped, and there were still approximately 1,000 people there, except those who had been killed and died. And column stopped. And this SS officer was on a back of a truck with a megaphone, and he told us you are in Riga, which is in Latvia, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and you are in the ghetto of Riga. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do we live? Where do we go? What's going on? What's happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: They designated a area to us behind that gate we walked in. And we saw, of course, the gate-- the extension of the gate was barbed wire. And there were houses in this barbed wire area. And they told us just go and find yourself a place to live. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Again, we had no worldly belongings other than what we had on our-- on our bodies and ourselves. And this was late in afternoon by now. It was getting dark and it was still cold. And everybody scrambled to get in one of the houses which were designated for us to move into. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as we got into these houses, they looked perfectly normal. There were beds and couches and sofas and chairs and tables. And what's surprising-- most surprising of all, there was food on the table [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the house we moved into. There was my father, my mother, my sister, my two aunts, myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was actually an apartment in a house where we moved into. And there were five other people aside from us who moved into this apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were no longer with the other ladies from Gemunden?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, we were sort of separated. As we got out of the train, the SS sort of moved us in one column, other people in another column. So we were not with those people from Gemunden anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In any event, we-- we came into this apartment. It was late in the afternoon. It was getting dark. And we see food on the table, food like bread, butter, ham, meat. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Open up the closets, there are potatoes in the closet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we couldn't figure out why-- why was that food there? Where did it come from? We knew the Germans was-- were not that generous or hospitable to us to give us a welcoming party.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after discussions-- discussions went on in our apartment, some of the older people and my father, the conclusion they arrived at, that the food was poisoned, that they put the food there because we were hungry, we hadn't eaten for several days, that we were ravished, we would consume the food and we would die. And nobody touched the food. Nobody ate anything. Food was left there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And nighttime came, we went to sleep exhausted. And the next morning, we got up and ventured out of the house we were in, and so did other people from our group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we found out that there was another group of German Jews in the ghetto, who just had arrived two days prior to our arrival. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were right in the next block, so to speak, from us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we found out from them that the food, which was left behind [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was food which was on a table from Latvian Jews who had lived in those houses prior to our arrival, two days prior to our arrival, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that they had been taken out of those houses, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and they had been shot. They had been killed. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And we figured out after that, you know, food was of prime importance. Shocked, unbelievable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we realized then that the food which was left behind was good food and that we could eat it. Everything was frozen, obviously. It was cold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But it wasn't kosher. Ham. What do you do with ham? You eat bread and you eat potatoes. You eat butter. But you don't eat ham.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the other people who lived in the same house with us weren't kosher. And we split up whatever food there was on the table between the butter and the bread. And we didn't-- my-- my mom, and dad and I, our family didn't touch any of the-- the meat, because it wasn't kosher. And we were just satisfied with whatever there was. A couple of days later, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the SS organized some of the more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] intellectual Jews of our group into a governing body. Within our group, one man was designated as the lageralteste. He was in charge of the group as a whole and responsible to the SS for the group. The SS organized work kommandos. And they designated someone else to be in charge of work kommandos. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They designated a group of people to be in charge of designating work kommandos, a typical German organized, orderly situation in an untenable, unlivable, horrendous environment. And we were told that we were going to be-- we were going to be designated to work, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and if we don't work, we won't eat, because each person was given a work card, which had to be-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] had to be stamped. And with this stamped card, you could get your ration when you came back from work. [SIGHS] [DRINKS] It didn't make any difference to us what kind of work we were doing. We were from the country. We were-- you know, we were from the land. We worked the land, so we were willing to work and do anything [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to stay alive and to earn some food. But as days progressed, we also found out that there was another ghetto [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] within the ghetto we were in, within the larger ghetto. And in this larger ghetto were Latvian Jews, which they kept separate from the German Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the people in the Latvian part of the ghetto were primarily men. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we found out the reason for it was that before we came, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans had rounded up all the women, all the children, all older people, and Those not capable of working, of Latvian Jews from Riga who had been, prior to our getting there, put into this ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] separated and segregated them, and taken 'em to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out to the woods [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and had shot them there, killed them there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course they killed. They killed-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everybody had-- and I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some stories that if it wouldn't have been for you German Jews who needed a place to live and to stay, those Latvian Jews would be still alive. But because you were coming in, they had to kill our people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's was a tremendous amount of anger, if not hate, from Latvian Jews [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to us German Jews. And we all basically had the same fate, you know. But it was the original-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] original reaction. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I might have probably reacted the same way if I would've been a Latvian Jew. As it turned out, the Latvian Jews later on became our very, very good friends when they realized that really we had nothing to do with their people being killed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Work kommandos were designated and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they-- they went-- can I stop a minute? I just can't go-- OK. So you and your father were working in the-- in [BACKROUND NOISE] the ghetto. Were the women also working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At that time, women we're working yet in the early days of the Riga ghetto. But my father was working. As a matter a fact, four days after our arrival in the ghetto, they organized groups to work outside the ghetto. My father was picked to work in Salaspils, which was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] outside the ghetto working [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] camp where Jews were taken to work in a-- in a lumber mill. That's what it was, a lumber mill. It had been worked in by Russian prisoners prior to the Jews arriving. It's really very, very hard, physical work. And these people who were taken to Salaspils didn't come back. They had to live there. And unbeknownst to us, after the first day they were taken out to Salaspils they didn't come back. And of course, we thought the worst had happened. But there was always a underground communication going on. And we found out that the men were all right, that they were working there. They were getting rations of food, but the work was very hard. It was in January already by then. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was in a-- in a work program, which lived in the ghetto. In other words, I still lived with my mother and my aunts and my sister. But I walked in a work [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] I used to call it, out of the ghetto every morning under SS guard. And I worked in a garage, in an automobile repair shop where there were SS cars being repaired and reconditioned. That was 1942. I was just about 15 years old, 16 years old. I was always interested in cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a matter of fact, I volunteered for that job. One morning, we were standing up out at the work assignment place, approximately 500 people. And they asked, who knows something about automobiles? And I raised my hand. I said I do. They choose me to be part of that group to go out and work on cars. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there wasn't that much hardship on that particular job. But then going back to the ghetto at night again, the hardship was so much worse, seeing the women suffer, and going to an environment which-- you know, like there was not going to be a tomorrow. This went on for quite a while. And by that time, my sister was-- she was a year younger than I was, a year and a half, so she must've been about 14 years old. She also was going to work camp and she was sorting clothes for the German Army in a [NON-ENGLISH], where they were repairing and-- and sorting clothes for German soldiers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Ghetto life, looking back at it compared to what came afterwards, was easy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It wasn't comfortable. It wasn't pleasant. But it was a life where you still had your family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where you had an opportunity to associate with other people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where you had a lot of information coming in through the grapevine, what was going on everywhere, including the rest of the world, because the Latvian Jews would go out to Riga and do the same or similar work we were doing. Those people had lived in Riga and they still had a lot of contacts. Not only did they have contacts, but they were able to organize groups we were-- which were able to bring food into the ghetto. And as I said earlier, as much as there was anger at first amongst the Latvian Jewish people who were left in the ghetto, in their ghetto, against the German Jews, slowly this relationship really thawed out and warmed up. And it became a really good relationship. Number one, there were very few women in the Latvian ghettos, and they were mostly men. Most of the German men, German Jewish men who had come into the ghetto were taken out of the ghetto and working outside the ghetto. A lot of young, young Jewish women, young girls. And the Latvian men who had had families and those who didn't have families were yearning for a relationship of some kind, a human relationship, whatever it entails, you know, emotional, physical. And the bond between the Latvian ghetto, Latvian Jews and the German Jews, just because of that kind of a need for each other, became very strong very quickly. And it was a good relationship. As a matter of fact, I remember after-- it must've been in middle of 1942, a young man, his name was Hirszke Kliksman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had-- he was a Kovno Jew who was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put into-- from Kovno into Riga, to Riga ghetto. And he lived with the Latvian men in the Latvian part of the ghetto. He worked in the same same work kommando my sister worked in, and they had become friendly, boyfriend and girlfriend. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he would come over to our house to visit my sister on Sundays when we didn't work.
